reviews.review_msg: Lots of very dense cogs readings that are hard to understand. This course was a tough one to get through. But it was fair with clear marking requirements. There was a group project. I got an amazing group however (your mileage may vary on that part). She was always very quick to respond to emails and answer any questions as well.

In the group project, two of us did most of the work. We lost a lot of points because of this. This was very unfair to those of us who put in the work. I wish the textbook was used, it would have made studying easier.

If you catch a marking mistake, she won't even bother replying to your email. This has been my least engaging course at SFU of my three years. She records all lectures, but she bothered posting for weeks 1, 2, and 8. Which is quite deceiving because that's the drop deadline. I wish I waited for another prof to teach this course.

COGS 200 with Prof Grant is fun and fairly involved. She covers a lot of material in the course with plenty of mandatory readings that can be quite time consuming. For a second-year level course it is not difficult but does demand a lot of time to be successful in. Prof. Grant is a solid lecturer and cares about the students.

There is only so much one can say about a professor like Shawn. His teaching style was odd and clunky as he spent a lot of time going over definitions for the most basic concepts. Exam questions make little sense and often are not properly proofread before going to print. Grading is incredibly unclear and harsh with no feedback.

Professor Tan is clearly passionate about what he teaches, however I think his delivery can be poor and it's hard to engage with his lectures. That being said, he makes everything you need to know very accessible. Attendance is not mandatory so easy to fit into your schedule. Just be prepared to take extra time to teach yourself a bit.

Took this class as an elective and it was a mistake. Only good thing is he posts the lecture recordings so you don't have to go to class. Instructions aren't always clear and the tests had grammar problems which affected the responses.. made it very confusing/stressful. Weekly quizzes and papers based on that week's chapter. Sooo much writing.
Very harsh grader with very vague instructions. He is a very nice man but as a prof he is not good. Pretty good but boring lectures, extremely nitpicky and unfair grading.

He is not good at teaching. He doesn't even stick by his own grading criteria and it's really difficult to know what he is expecting. When students inevitably struggle he is not caring enough to be helpful.
far from clear presentations and slides, can't explain concepts clearly but expects students to be able to do that.

Super super super nice lady. Extremely incompetent prof. Memorize slides/summaries of readings and spew it out on exams/papers; you don't need to show understanding of any kind just write as much BS as you can and you get handed an A+! So weird but it works.
Tough homework assignments, would advise asking for help on these. Other than that attend all classes and you should get a great grade.
He is great nowadays. He is underrated here.

Lectures consistent of student presentations of an article every week and the rest of the class is him talking about a subject he wants to. Things were not well organized, and hard to study for. An 80 was considered a C and we did not know this at all. He is a very nice guy though and I liked him as a person. Though over all not a very hard class.

He's a good guy, unfortunately not in COGS himself so he can't speak to a lot of the CS or Psyc topics, engaging and teaches well though. Vague in marking as everyone else says but you can also just ask him questions over email or before/after class and he will answer. He's reasonable and if you have issues with group mates you can talk to him.

Good lecturer, frequent class discussions and group work are interesting and fun. However he is very unclear on his marking scheme. You can get Bs and As on all midterms and presentations based on his scheme and end up with a C- final grade - other components supposidly being worth a combined 20%. States this is standard practice.
